title: Deploy to a VPS
description: Get your FastAPI app running on a real server with nginx, systemd, and HTTPS.

steps.title: What you need
steps.instruction:
## Getting a server

A **VPS** (Virtual Private Server) is a Linux machine you rent by the month. Your app runs on it 24/7, accessible to anyone on the internet.

### Choosing a provider

| Provider | Entry price | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hetzner | ~$4/mo | Cheapest, European data centres |
| DigitalOcean | ~$6/mo | Good documentation, popular |
| Linode (Akamai) | ~$5/mo | Reliable, long-running |

Any of these will work. For a FastAPI todo app, the cheapest tier (1 CPU, 1 GB RAM) is more than enough.

### SSH keys

You connect to the server via **SSH** - a secure terminal connection. Instead of a password, you use a key pair: a private key that stays on your machine and a public key that goes on the server.

```bash
# Generate a key pair (do this once on your local machine)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@email.com"

# This creates two files:
# ~/.ssh/id_ed25519       (private key - never share this)
# ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub   (public key - this goes on the server)
```

When you create a VPS, the provider asks for your public key. Paste the contents of `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`.

### What you need before continuing

1. A VPS with Ubuntu 22.04 (or 24.04) - any provider works
2. Your SSH public key added during server creation
3. The server's IP address from your provider dashboard

---

**Your turn:** Generate an SSH key pair on your local machine.
steps.starter_code:
# Generate an SSH key pair (run on your LOCAL machine, not the server)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@email.com"

# View your public key to copy it to your VPS provider
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
steps.solution_code:
# Generate an SSH key pair (run on your LOCAL machine, not the server)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@email.com"

# View your public key to copy it to your VPS provider
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
steps.hint: The commands are already written - read through them and press Check to continue.

steps.title: First server setup
steps.instruction:
## Getting the server ready

Once the VPS is created, connect to it:

```bash
# Replace 1.2.3.4 with your server's IP address
ssh root@1.2.3.4
```

You are now in a terminal on your server. Everything you type runs there, not on your laptop.

### Update the system

Always do this first on a fresh server. It installs security patches:

```bash
apt update && apt upgrade -y
```

### Install Python and tools

```bash
apt install -y python3 python3-pip python3-venv git nginx
```

- `python3-venv` - needed to create virtual environments
- `git` - needed to clone your project
- `nginx` - the web server that will sit in front of your app (covered later)

### Create a non-root user (recommended)

Running your app as root is a security risk. Create a normal user:

```bash
adduser deploy
usermod -aG sudo deploy
```

Then reconnect as that user:
```bash
ssh deploy@1.2.3.4
```

---

**Your turn:** Connect to your server and install Python, git, and nginx.
steps.starter_code:
# Connect to the server
ssh root@YOUR_SERVER_IP

# Update system packages
apt update && apt upgrade -y

# Install Python, git, and nginx
apt install -y python3 python3-pip python3-venv git nginx

# Create a non-root user
adduser deploy
usermod -aG sudo deploy
steps.solution_code:
# Connect to the server
ssh root@YOUR_SERVER_IP

# Update system packages
apt update && apt upgrade -y

# Install Python, git, and nginx
apt install -y python3 python3-pip python3-venv git nginx

# Create a non-root user
adduser deploy
usermod -aG sudo deploy

steps.title: Deploying your code
steps.instruction:
## Getting your app onto the server

The simplest way to get your code on the server is `git clone`.

First, push your project to GitHub (covered in the Git course). Then on the server:

```bash
cd /home/deploy
git clone https://github.com/yourname/yourproject.git
cd yourproject
```

### Set up a virtual environment

```bash
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### Create the .env file

Your `.env` file is in `.gitignore`, so it was not cloned. You need to create it manually on the server:

```bash
nano .env
```

Add your production environment variables:
```
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:password@localhost/myapp
SECRET_KEY=your-long-random-string
```

Generate a secure SECRET_KEY:
```bash
python3 -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))"
```

### Test that it starts

```bash
source venv/bin/activate
uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
```

Visit `http://YOUR_SERVER_IP:8000` - if you see your app, the code is working. Press `Ctrl+C` to stop it - the next step sets it up to run automatically.

---

**Your turn:** Clone your project, set up the virtual environment, and create the .env file.
steps.starter_code:
# Clone your project
cd /home/deploy
git clone https://github.com/yourname/yourproject.git
cd yourproject

# Create virtual environment and install dependencies
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Create the .env file with production values
nano .env

# Test that the app starts
uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
steps.solution_code:
# Clone your project
cd /home/deploy
git clone https://github.com/yourname/yourproject.git
cd yourproject

# Create virtual environment and install dependencies
python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Create the .env file with production values
nano .env

# Test that the app starts
uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000
steps.hint: The commands are already written - read through them and press Check to continue.

steps.title: Running with systemd
steps.instruction:
## Keeping your app running

Right now your app stops the moment you close the terminal or the server reboots. **systemd** is Linux's process manager - it starts services on boot and restarts them if they crash.

### Create a service file

Create `/etc/systemd/system/myapp.service`:

```bash
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service
```

Paste this content:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=My FastAPI App
After=network.target

[Service]
User=deploy
WorkingDirectory=/home/deploy/yourproject
ExecStart=/home/deploy/yourproject/venv/bin/uvicorn main:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000
Restart=always
EnvironmentFile=/home/deploy/yourproject/.env

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Key parts:
- `ExecStart` - the full path to uvicorn inside your venv, and the command to run
- `Restart=always` - systemd restarts the app if it crashes
- `EnvironmentFile` - loads your `.env` file so the app has its config
- `--host 127.0.0.1` - listen on localhost only; nginx will forward traffic from port 80

### Enable and start the service

```bash
sudo systemctl daemon-reload     # reload systemd after creating the file
sudo systemctl enable myapp      # start automatically on boot
sudo systemctl start myapp       # start it now
sudo systemctl status myapp      # check it's running
```

### View logs

```bash
sudo journalctl -u myapp -f      # follow the logs in real time
```

---

**Your turn:** Write the systemd service file for your app.
steps.starter_code:
[Unit]
Description=My FastAPI App
After=network.target

[Service]
User=deploy
WorkingDirectory=/home/deploy/yourproject
ExecStart=/home/deploy/yourproject/venv/bin/uvicorn main:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000
Restart=always
EnvironmentFile=/home/deploy/yourproject/.env

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
steps.solution_code:
[Unit]
Description=My FastAPI App
After=network.target

[Service]
User=deploy
WorkingDirectory=/home/deploy/yourproject
ExecStart=/home/deploy/yourproject/venv/bin/uvicorn main:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000
Restart=always
EnvironmentFile=/home/deploy/yourproject/.env

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

steps.title: nginx as a reverse proxy
steps.instruction:
## Why nginx

Your app listens on port 8000, but web browsers expect port 80 (HTTP) and 443 (HTTPS). **nginx** sits in front and forwards traffic:

```
Browser → port 80/443 → nginx → port 8000 → your app
```

nginx also handles SSL termination, compression, and static file serving - things uvicorn is not designed for.

### Create a site config

```bash
sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/myapp
```

```nginx
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name yourdomain.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8000;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
    }
}
```

- `proxy_pass` - forward all requests to your uvicorn process
- `proxy_set_header` - pass the original request headers through so your app sees the real client IP

### Enable the site

```bash
# Link it into sites-enabled
sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/myapp /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/

# Test the config for syntax errors
sudo nginx -t

# Reload nginx to apply the change
sudo systemctl reload nginx
```

Visit `http://yourdomain.com` - your app should respond.

---

**Your turn:** Write the nginx site config for your app.
steps.hint: The nginx config is already written - read through it and press Check to continue.

steps.title: HTTPS with Certbot
steps.instruction:
## Adding HTTPS

HTTPS encrypts traffic between the browser and your server. Every app that handles user data needs it. Without it, passwords and tokens travel in plain text.

**Certbot** is a free tool from Let's Encrypt. It obtains a TLS certificate, installs it in nginx, and sets up automatic renewal.

### Install Certbot

```bash
sudo apt install -y certbot python3-certbot-nginx
```

### Obtain a certificate

```bash
sudo certbot --nginx -d yourdomain.com
```

Certbot will:
1. Verify you own the domain by making a request to it (this is why the domain must already point to your server's IP)
2. Download a certificate from Let's Encrypt
3. Automatically update your nginx config to add HTTPS and redirect HTTP to HTTPS

### Test auto-renewal

Certificates expire after 90 days. Certbot installs a cron job or systemd timer to renew automatically. Test that it would work:

```bash
sudo certbot renew --dry-run
```

If it says "Congratulations, all simulated renewals succeeded", you are done.

### The full picture

Your deployment stack now looks like:

```
Browser
  ↓ HTTPS (port 443)
nginx  ←  Certbot manages the certificate
  ↓ HTTP (127.0.0.1:8000)
uvicorn (managed by systemd)
  ↓
PostgreSQL (or SQLite)
```

This is the same architecture used by most production web applications.

---

**Your turn:** Install Certbot and obtain an HTTPS certificate for your domain.
steps.starter_code:
# Install Certbot and the nginx plugin
sudo apt install -y certbot python3-certbot-nginx

# Obtain a certificate (replace yourdomain.com with your actual domain)
sudo certbot --nginx -d yourdomain.com

# Test automatic renewal
sudo certbot renew --dry-run
steps.solution_code:
# Install Certbot and the nginx plugin
sudo apt install -y certbot python3-certbot-nginx

# Obtain a certificate (replace yourdomain.com with your actual domain)
sudo certbot --nginx -d yourdomain.com

# Test automatic renewal
sudo certbot renew --dry-run

steps.title: Docker basics
steps.instruction:
## Packaging your app for anywhere

You have deployed your app to a VPS using systemd and a virtual environment. That works well. But as teams and projects grow, you often hear: "it works on my machine." Docker solves this by packaging your app and its entire environment  -  Python version, system libraries, dependencies  -  into a single portable unit called a **container**.

A container runs identically on your laptop, your CI system, and your production server.

### The Dockerfile

A `Dockerfile` is a recipe for building an image:

```dockerfile
# Start from the official Python image
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Set the working directory inside the container
WORKDIR /app

# Copy dependency list first (Docker caches layers)
COPY requirements.txt .

# Install dependencies
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy the rest of your code
COPY . .

# Tell Docker which port the app listens on
EXPOSE 8000

# The command to start the app
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
```

### Building and running

```bash
# Build the image (run from the folder containing Dockerfile)
docker build -t myapp .

# Run a container from the image
docker run -p 8000:8000 myapp
```

`-p 8000:8000` maps port 8000 on your machine to port 8000 inside the container.

### docker-compose for multiple services

Real apps have multiple pieces  -  your FastAPI app, a PostgreSQL database, Redis. `docker-compose.yml` defines them all and starts them together:

```yaml
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - '8000:8000'
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
  redis:
    image: redis:7
```

Start everything with one command: `docker compose up`

---

**Your turn:** Write the Dockerfile for your FastAPI app.
steps.starter_code:
# Start from the official slim Python image
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Copy and install dependencies first (better layer caching)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy the rest of the source code
COPY . .

# Document the port
EXPOSE 8000

# Start the app
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
steps.solution_code:
FROM python:3.12-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

EXPOSE 8000

CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
steps.hint: The Dockerfile is already written - read through it and press Check to continue.
